regulator: MAS (Monetary Authority of Singapore) -- sole regulator of this pillar; IRAS (Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore) has a real but strictly ancillary role limited to the tax (GST and income tax) treatment of digital tokens, not licensing or prudential regulation of stablecoin issuance itself
instruments: MAS media release, 'MAS Finalises Stablecoin Regulatory Framework', Singapore, 15 August 2023 -- announces the completed design (not yet the legal enactment) of a dedicated regulatory framework for single-currency stablecoins (SCS) pegged to the Singapore Dollar or any G10 currency and issued in Singapore, covering value stability of reserve assets, minimum base capital and liquid assets, redemption of par value within five business days, and user disclosure, plus an opt-in 'MAS-regulated stablecoin' label with misrepresentation penalties and Investor Alert List placement for false claims; confirmed by direct fetch of the MAS media release PDF as mirrored, genuinely, on the Singapore Government's official press-release repository, since every path on mas.gov.sg itself -- including this exact PDF's own -/media/ URL -- returns an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' HTML shell rather than the document, live-verified in this research session

Payment Services Act 2019 (Act 2 of 2019, 'PS Act') -- the general activity-based licensing statute administered by MAS that today governs stablecoin-adjacent activity in the absence of the dedicated SCS regime described above; widely and consistently reported (including by MAS's own April 2024 media release referenced below, and independently by multiple Singapore law-firm alerts) as having commenced 28 January 2020, though this research could not itself directly fetch a primary MAS or Singapore Statutes Online page confirming that exact commencement date this session -- see open_items

MAS media release, 'MAS Expands Scope of Regulated Payment Services; Introduces User Protection Requirements for Digital Payment Token Service Providers', Singapore, 2 April 2024 -- amendments to the PS Act and its subsidiary legislation, taking effect in stages from 4 April 2024, bringing DPT custody, DPT transmission/exchange facilitation (even without possession of the DPTs), and certain cross-border money transfer facilitation within MAS's licensing scope, and empowering MAS to impose AML/CFT, user-protection and financial-stability requirements on DPT service providers; the amended safeguarding-of-customer-assets regulations (segregation, trust accounts) took effect six months later, i.e. from 4 October 2024; this instrument governs any stablecoin activity currently characterised as a 'digital payment token service' under the PS Act, pending the dedicated SCS regime -- confirmed by direct fetch of the MAS media release PDF, again only fetchable via its Singapore Government press-release mirror rather than mas.gov.sg itself; full detail on the custody/transmission scope expansion is tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar, not duplicated here

IRAS e-Tax Guide, 'GST: Digital Payment Tokens' (Third Edition, published 30 January 2026; first edition 19 November 2019; second edition 3 August 2022) -- confirmed by direct fetch of the current PDF from iras.gov.sg; sets out that, with effect from 1 January 2020, supplies of 'digital payment tokens' (DPTs) as defined in the GST Act are exempt from GST (no GST on their use as payment, and exchange/loan of DPTs for fiat or other DPTs is an exempt supply); the guide's own Glossary (s.3.2) defines a 'digital payment token' as, among other characteristics, a token that 'is not denominated in any currency, and is not pegged by its issuer to any currency' -- a definitional element that, on its face, would place a currency-pegged stablecoin outside the DPT category for GST purposes specifically; this research did not independently confirm, via direct fetch of the PS Act's own operative text (sso.agc.gov.sg block this session, see open_items), whether the PS Act's licensing-scope definition of 'digital payment token' contains the identical 'not pegged by its issuer' limb, so this card does not assert that the GST Act's DPT boundary and the PS Act's licensing-scope DPT boundary are identical -- see open_items

Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg) -- the Attorney-General's Chambers' official portal for the consolidated text of the Payment Services Act 2019 and the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022; registered here as a key_link for identification purposes only. Live-verified in this research session: every path tried (the Payment Services Act's own Acts-Supp listing page, and other paths tested in this project's config registration pass) returns an identical HTTP 403 CloudFront error page ('ERROR: The request could not be satisfied ... Request blocked'), so no PS Act or FSM Act statutory text could be directly fetched and quoted this session -- see open_items
standing_summary: Singapore has no stablecoin-specific statute in force today. MAS -- simultaneously Singapore's central bank and its single integrated financial regulator, with no separate AML, securities, or stablecoin-specific regulatory body -- announced the completed design of a dedicated regulatory framework for single-currency stablecoins (SCS) on 15 August 2023, following an October 2022 public consultation, but per MAS's own media release, that framework is a description of finalised policy requirements, not yet an enacted law: this research directly fetched the 15 August 2023 media release (mirrored on the Singapore Government's official press-release repository, since mas.gov.sg itself blocks every path tested, including this exact document's own URL, behind an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' HTML shell) and confirmed its stated scope and requirements verbatim. The framework applies only to SCS pegged to the Singapore Dollar or any G10 currency and issued in Singapore, and would require issuers to meet requirements on: value stability (composition, valuation, custody and audit of reserve assets); capital (minimum base capital and liquid assets for orderly wind-down); redemption at par (return of par value within five business days of a redemption request); and disclosure (of the stabilising mechanism, holder rights, and reserve audit results). Only issuers meeting all requirements may apply to have their token recognised and labelled an 'MAS-regulated stablecoin' -- a status intended to let users distinguish it from other, unregulated 'stablecoins' -- and misrepresenting a token as MAS-regulated can draw financial penalties or imprisonment and placement on MAS's Investor Alert List. As of this card's generation date (13 July 2026), this research live-confirmed the negative: no bill to give the SCS framework legal effect has yet been introduced to the Singapore Parliament. A direct fetch of Parliament's own 'Bills Introduced' listing page (parliament.gov.sg) today showed the ten most recently introduced bills -- none titled or evidently related to the Payment Services Act, the Financial Services and Markets Act, or stablecoins (the most relevant recent entry being an unrelated Securities and Futures (Amendment) Bill). This is consistent with MAS's own public statements reported in trade press: MAS Managing Director Chia Der Jiun said at the Singapore FinTech Festival on 13 November 2025 that MAS was 'ready to begin drafting stablecoin legislation', with the framework separately reported as targeted to take legal effect around mid-2026 -- a target this card's live Parliament check shows has not yet been met by an introduced bill as of 13 July 2026 (secondary-sourced statement, not independently fetched from a MAS primary transcript this session -- see open_items). In the interim, and in the continued absence of MAS's own PS Act text (sso.agc.gov.sg returns a live-verified HTTP 403 on every path tested, and mas.gov.sg returns the same Maintenance block described above), stablecoin-type tokens fall to be regulated, if at all, under the PS Act's general 'digital payment token' service-provider licensing (major or standard payment institution) and/or 'e-money' issuance categories, the same activity-based regime that governs exchanges generally (tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar) and that was itself expanded on 2 April 2024 -- confirmed by direct fetch of that MAS media release, again reachable only via its Singapore Government press-release mirror -- to bring DPT custody and DPT transmission/exchange facilitation within MAS's licensing scope, with staged effect from 4 April 2024 and safeguarding-of-customer-assets rules from 4 October 2024 (full detail tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar). A genuine boundary question this research surfaced but could not fully resolve: IRAS's own e-Tax Guide on GST treatment of digital payment tokens (Third Edition, published 30 January 2026, directly fetched from iras.gov.sg) defines a 'digital payment token' for GST-exemption purposes as, among other things, a token that is 'not denominated in any currency, and is not pegged by its issuer to any currency' -- on its face excluding currency-pegged stablecoins from that GST-exempt DPT category. Whether the PS Act's own licensing-scope definition of 'digital payment token' contains the identical carve-out (which would mean formally issuer-pegged stablecoins fall instead under the PS Act's separate 'e-money' category, while informally-stabilised or algorithmically-stabilised tokens without a formal issuer peg remain DPTs) could not be confirmed this session because sso.agc.gov.sg -- Singapore's official statute portal -- blocks every path tested with an HTTP 403, and mas.gov.sg is likewise blocked; this card therefore states the IRAS definition only for what IRAS's own document says, and does not extend it to the PS Act by assumption. MAS is also pursuing stablecoins as one strand of broader settlement-asset work outside a licensing context: its 16 October 2025 media release launching the BLOOM (Borderless, Liquid, Open, Online, Multi-currency) initiative -- directly fetched from its Singapore Government press-release mirror -- describes MAS convening industry participants to standardise settlement using 'tokenised bank liabilities and well-regulated stablecoins', building on Project Orchid; this is an industry-collaboration initiative, not a licensing or authorisation regime, and its wholesale-CBDC and tokenised-money strands proper are tracked in the Banking & Money pillar, not duplicated here. Two negatives register cleanly on live evidence gathered this session: the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority (ACRA) has no independent digital-asset regulatory role beyond ordinary company registration (a stablecoin issuer, like any company, must incorporate via ACRA, but ACRA does not itself license, supervise or set requirements for stablecoin activity), and the Singapore Police Force's Commercial Affairs Department (CAD) is a criminal-investigation body -- the recipient, alongside MAS, of suspicious-transaction reports and the agency that pursues fraud and money-laundering cases -- not a stablecoin regulator; MAS remains the sole regulator of this pillar.

key_links.label: MAS media release (via Singapore Government press-release repository, since mas.gov.sg itself blocks this document): MAS Finalises Stablecoin Regulatory Framework (15 Aug 2023)

MAS media release (via Singapore Government press-release repository): MAS Expands Scope of Regulated Payment Services; Introduces User Protection Requirements for Digital Payment Token Service Providers (2 Apr 2024)
MAS media release (via Singapore Government press-release repository): MAS Launches BLOOM Initiative to Extend Settlement Capabilities (16 Oct 2025)
IRAS e-Tax Guide: GST -- Digital Payment Tokens (Third Edition, published 30 Jan 2026)
IRAS: Digital Payment Tokens (GST guidance landing page)
Singapore Parliament: Bills Introduced (live listing, checked for any stablecoin/PS Act/FSM Act bill as of this card's generation date)

Singapore Statutes Online: Payment Services Act 2019 (link-listing only -- live-verified this session as returning an HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tested, so its text could not be fetched or quoted)

MAS: MAS Finalises Stablecoin Regulatory Framework (mas.gov.sg original URL -- live-verified this session as blocked, serving an 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' page instead of the release; listed for reference only, not used as a fetch source)

open_items: The dedicated single-currency stablecoin (SCS) regulatory framework MAS announced on 15 August 2023 (value stability, capital, redemption-at-par, disclosure requirements, and the 'MAS-regulated stablecoin' label) is, by MAS's own description in that release, a finalised policy design awaiting legislative amendments before it takes legal effect. This research live-confirmed, via a direct fetch of Singapore Parliament's own 'Bills Introduced' listing on the card's generation date (13 July 2026), that no such bill has yet been introduced. Trade press has reported MAS's Managing Director stating in November 2025 that MAS was ready to begin drafting the legislation, with a mid-2026 target for the framework to take legal effect; this research did not independently fetch a MAS primary transcript of that statement (mas.gov.sg is blocked; the statement is sourced here to secondary trade-press reporting only) and could not confirm any bill introduction, so status_seal is set to 'proposed' rather than 'bill_pending' or 'in_force'. This should be re-checked against Parliament's Bills Introduced page and MAS's own output once fetchable.

This research could not directly fetch or quote the operative text of the Payment Services Act 2019 or the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 this session: sso.agc.gov.sg (Singapore Statutes Online, the official statute portal) returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tested, and mas.gov.sg -- which also publishes PS Act guidance/FAQ PDFs -- returned an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' HTML page on every path tested, including direct -/media/ PDF URLs. Consequently: (a) the PS Act's own statutory definitions of 'digital payment token' and 'e-money', and specifically whether the PS Act definition of DPT contains the same 'not pegged by its issuer to any currency' exclusion found in IRAS's GST Act-based e-Tax Guide definition, could not be independently confirmed -- this card states the IRAS definition only for the GST Act and does not extend it to the PS Act by assumption; and (b) the Payment Services Act 2019's exact commencement date (widely reported by multiple independent secondary/law-firm sources, and consistent with MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release footnote referencing the 'Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021' as amending an already-commenced PS Act, as 28 January 2020) is stated in this card's instruments list as secondary-corroborated background only, not as an independently primary-source-fetched fact.

Which regulatory category (DPT service under the PS Act, e-money issuance under the PS Act, or a capital-markets product under the Securities and Futures Act, where a token's structure brings it within SFA scope) currently applies, as a matter of MAS's own stated interim treatment, to a specific stablecoin design pending the dedicated SCS framework's legal effect, could not be confirmed from a MAS primary statement addressing that interim classification question directly; this research found only the general PS Act DPT/e-money definitional structure (via the IRAS e-Tax Guide, a GST Act document) and MAS's 2 April 2024 media release on DPT custody/transmission scope expansion, neither of which is itself a statement about stablecoin interim classification specifically.

MAS Managing Director Chia Der Jiun's reported November 2025 Singapore FinTech Festival remarks on stablecoin legislation timing are sourced in this card only to secondary trade-press reporting (a Central Banking magazine article), not to a directly fetched MAS speech transcript or press release; mas.gov.sg's speeches section is blocked in the same manner as its media-releases section, and this research did not locate a Singapore Government press-release-repository mirror of that specific speech this session.

The Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 Part 9 digital token service provider (DTSP) regime, which commenced 30 June 2025 and imposes AML/CFT licensing on entities providing token-related services only to customers outside Singapore, is not detailed in this card because its officially stated scope and rationale (FATF alignment, cross-border money-laundering risk) are AML/CFT-enforcement-specific rather than stablecoin-specific; it is tracked in the AML/CFT & Enforcement pillar. Whether any DTSP-regime obligations apply in practice to a stablecoin issuer or intermediary specifically was not confirmed this session and is not asserted here.

This card's confirmation that the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority (ACRA) and the Singapore Police Force's Commercial Affairs Department (CAD) have no independent digital-asset regulatory role (as opposed to, respectively, general company registration and criminal investigation) is based on secondary corroboration (multiple independent legal/compliance summaries and the CAD's own general enforcement-mandate description on Singapore Government sites) rather than a single primary MAS or ACRA statement addressing digital assets directly; treated here as a well-corroborated negative rather than a directly quoted primary-source fact.
